issues.description: All controllers use console.log(error) for error handling. This is the standard AutoBE pattern but does not provide structured logging with context (user ID, request ID, timestamp, severity level).
issues.suggestion: Consider integrating a structured logging library (e.g., Winston, Pino) with request context propagation for production environments.

issues.description: No rate limiting is applied to authentication endpoints (join, login, refresh). These endpoints are susceptible to brute-force and credential stuffing attacks without rate limiting.
issues.suggestion: Apply rate limiting middleware (e.g., @nestjs/throttler) especially on login and refresh endpoints to mitigate brute-force attacks.

issues.description: Password complexity validation is not enforced at the controller/DTO level. The IJoin body type should enforce minimum password strength requirements.
issues.suggestion: Add password complexity rules (minimum length, character classes) via typia validation tags or a custom validation pipe on the IJoin body.

issues.description: All report endpoints (create, index, at, update, erase) lack authentication guards. The spec comments note '@x-autobe-authorization-type null' and '@x-autobe-authorization-actor null', meaning no NestJS Guard is applied. These endpoints manage sensitive organizational report definitions including creation, retrieval, update, and deletion. Without a guard, unauthenticated requests can reach the provider layer, which must then handle auth internally — but there is no visible enforcement at the controller level.
issues.suggestion: Apply an appropriate NestJS authentication guard (e.g., @UseGuards(AuthGuard)) to the controller class or individual sensitive methods. The provider-level authorization logic described in the spec comments should be a second layer, not the only layer.

issues.description: Both employee endpoints (index and at) have '@x-autobe-authorization-actor null' and no visible authentication guard at the controller level. These endpoints expose employee directory data including personal information, roles, and department assignments. Without a guard, the authorization logic relies entirely on the provider layer with no controller-level enforcement.
issues.suggestion: Apply an authentication guard at the controller or method level to ensure only authenticated users can access employee directory data. Organization-scoped permission checks in the provider are necessary but should not be the sole enforcement point.

issues.description: All controllers use console.log(error) for error handling across all endpoints. This is the standard AutoBE pattern but provides no structured logging, log levels, correlation IDs, or audit trail for security-relevant events such as unauthorized access attempts or deletion of report definitions.
issues.suggestion: Consider replacing console.log with a structured logger (e.g., NestJS Logger or Winston) that supports log levels, correlation IDs, and can be configured to send logs to a centralized monitoring system.

issues.description: No rate limiting is applied to any of these endpoints. Report creation, listing, and deletion endpoints could be abused by automated clients to enumerate or exhaust resources.
issues.suggestion: Apply rate limiting at the API gateway or using a NestJS throttler guard (e.g., @nestjs/throttler) to protect against abuse.

issues.description: The activity log query uses a random timestamp (typia.random) to find 'active' owner sessions, and then enforces a single-session constraint. This is a flawed access control mechanism — the random timestamp means the query result is non-deterministic and could return sessions from any time window. Any caller can access activity logs without proper authentication; there is no JWT/token validation or user identity check.
issues.suggestion: Replace the random timestamp with the actual current time (new Date().toISOString()) and require a proper authenticated owner/manager payload with JWT verification, similar to other providers in this codebase.

issues.description: This endpoint retrieves task history records without any authentication or authorization check. No user payload (owner, manager, or employee) is required, and no session validation is performed. Any unauthenticated caller can enumerate task histories across all projects.
issues.suggestion: Add an authenticated user payload parameter and validate the caller's session and organization membership before returning task history data.

issues.description: This endpoint updates project memberships (including reassigning employees) without any authentication or authorization check. No user payload is required, and no session validation is performed. Any unauthenticated caller can modify project memberships.
issues.suggestion: Add an authenticated manager or owner payload parameter and validate the caller's session and organization membership before allowing membership updates.

issues.description: This endpoint returns full manager profile data (including email and other PII) without any authentication check. No user payload is required. Any unauthenticated caller can retrieve manager details by guessing or enumerating UUIDs.
issues.suggestion: Add an authenticated user payload and verify the caller has permission to view manager details before returning the data.

issues.description: The owner payload is accepted but there is no validation that the owner's session is active, not expired, or that the owner account is not deleted/deactivated before allowing organization creation. Other owner endpoints perform these checks.
issues.suggestion: Add session validity and account status checks (deleted_at, deactivated_at) before allowing the operation, consistent with other owner-authenticated endpoints.

issues.description: The owner payload is accepted but there is no validation that the organization belongs to the authenticated owner. Any authenticated owner can retrieve any organization by ID, enabling cross-tenant data access.
issues.suggestion: Add a check that the organization is associated with the authenticated owner's account before returning organization data.

issues.description: The manager payload is accepted with 'void props.manager' — the manager identity is completely ignored. No session validation, no organization scoping, and no check that the employee belongs to the manager's organization. Any authenticated manager can list contracts for any employee.
issues.suggestion: Remove the 'void props.manager' pattern and implement proper session validation and organization-scoped access control to ensure managers can only access employees within their organization.

issues.description: An employee is allowed to approve and reject their own timesheets (status transitions 'submitted' -> 'approved' and 'submitted' -> 'rejected' are permitted for the employee). Timesheet approval should be a manager/owner action, not a self-service operation.
issues.suggestion: Restrict 'approved' and 'rejected' status transitions to manager or owner roles only. Employees should only be able to submit (draft -> submitted) or retract (rejected -> submitted).

issues.description: The organization lookup for new top-level departments uses findFirstOrThrow without any organization scoping to the manager's context. It retrieves the first non-deleted organization in the database, which could be any organization, not necessarily the one the manager belongs to.
issues.suggestion: Scope the organization lookup to the manager's assigned organization ID, retrieved from the manager's session or profile.

issues.description: Session validation does not check session expiry (expired_at) or logout status (logged_out_at) before allowing timer creation, unlike putHrmTimeTrackingEmployeeTimelogsTimelogId which does perform these checks.
issues.suggestion: Add expired_at and logged_out_at checks to the session validation, consistent with other employee-authenticated endpoints.

issues.description: Token refresh operations and authentication failures are not logged. Failed refresh attempts (invalid tokens, deleted/deactivated accounts) should be recorded for security monitoring.
issues.suggestion: Add structured audit logging for authentication events including failed refresh attempts, account status rejections, and successful token refreshes.

issues.description: The pagination limit has no maximum cap — a caller can set limit to an arbitrarily large number (e.g., 1,000,000), potentially causing excessive database load and memory consumption.
issues.suggestion: Enforce a maximum limit (e.g., 500 or 1000) on all paginated endpoints to prevent resource exhaustion.

issues.description: The manager approval endpoint does not validate that the manager belongs to the same organization as the timesheet being approved. Any authenticated manager can approve timesheets from any organization.
issues.suggestion: Add an organization membership check: verify that the manager is associated with the same hrm_time_tracking_organization_id as the timesheet before allowing approval.

issues.description: The employee timelog creation does not validate that the project belongs to the same organization as the employee. An employee could log time against projects from other organizations.
issues.suggestion: After fetching the project, verify that the employee is a member of the organization associated with the project (project.hrm_time_tracking_organization_id).

issues.description: Sensitive operations such as contract deletion lack audit logging. There is no record of who deleted what and when beyond the deleted_at timestamp.
issues.suggestion: Add structured audit logging for destructive operations, recording the manager ID, employee ID, contract ID, and timestamp.

issues.description: The pagination limit has no maximum cap (defaults to 100 but can be set arbitrarily high by the client), which could lead to excessive data retrieval or denial of service.
issues.suggestion: Enforce a maximum limit value (e.g., Math.min(props.body.limit ?? 100, 100)) to prevent clients from requesting unbounded result sets.

agentEvaluations.summary: [sampled 30/205 files] [4 chunks, 24→23 issues] The codebase demonstrates good security practices overall: authentication endpoints use typed bodies with typia validation, role-based access control decorators (OwnerAuth, ManagerAuth) are consistently applied to protected endpoints, organization-scoped isolation is enforced at the provider level per the specifications, and UUID format validation is applied to path parameters. The main concerns are infrastructure-level: missing rate limiting on auth endpoints, absence of structured logging, and no password complexity enforcement — all standard AutoBE limitations. No critical injection, IDOR, or cryptographic vulnerabilities are visible at the controller layer.

issues.description: The code references `owner.sessions[0].organization.id` but the Prisma query selects `organization: { select: { id: true } }` under `sessions`. This creates a nested structure `owner.sessions[0].organization.id` which is valid, but the session creation later uses `owner.sessions[0].organization.id` to connect organization. However, the session query includes `organization` selection, but the session creation assumes the owner has an organization through their first session, which is an unrealistic assumption for a new login. The owner might not have any existing sessions.
issues.suggestion: Review the business logic: an owner should be associated with an organization through a different relationship (e.g., ownership). The session creation should connect to the owner's organization via a proper relationship, not via the last session.

issues.description: The login logic incorrectly requires an existing session for authentication. The query includes `owner.sessions[0] === undefined` as a condition for throwing "Invalid credentials". This means an owner cannot log in if they have no existing sessions, which is illogical for a first-time login.
issues.suggestion: Remove the check for `owner.sessions[0] === undefined` from the authentication condition. Only check password and account status.

issues.description: The refresh token verification uses `typia.assert` on the JWT payload, but the JWT payload may not match the expected structure (e.g., missing fields). The `typia.assert` will throw, but the catch block returns `throwUnauthorized()`. However, the `typia.assert` is applied to the result of `jwt.verify`, which returns `string | JwtPayload`. If it's a string, `typia.assert` will fail. This is fragile.
issues.suggestion: Use a type guard or explicit casting after verifying the token structure exists, similar to the owner refresh implementation.

issues.description: The token generation includes a redundant check `now.constructor === Date` which is always true because `now` is a Date object. The ternary operator is unnecessary and could be simplified.
issues.suggestion: Simplify token generation: use `expiredAt.toISOString()` and `refreshableUntil.toISOString()` directly without the ternary check.

issues.description: Multiple authentication provider files (owner, manager, employee) share similar patterns but with inconsistent error handling and token validation. For example, owner refresh uses explicit checks, manager uses `typia.assert`, employee uses manual property checks. This is a copy-paste pattern with inconsistent modifications.
issues.suggestion: Standardize token validation across all actor types using a shared utility function to ensure consistency.

issues.description: Session creation includes empty strings for `href` and `referrer` fields. These are likely placeholders for actual tracking data.
issues.suggestion: Consider passing actual href and referrer from request headers or default to null if not required.

issues.description: The employee login assumes a built-in role named "Employee" exists and is unique. It fetches the first built-in role with name "Employee" without ensuring it belongs to the employee's organization.
issues.suggestion: The role should be fetched based on the employee's assignment (e.g., through Employee->Role relationship). The current approach may return a role from a different organization.

agentEvaluations.summary: [11 chunks, 328→75 issues] Code shows consistent patterns but has critical hallucination issues with Prisma imports across all files. Several logic errors and incomplete implementations exist, particularly in employee filter updates and owner profile updates. The code structure follows AutoBE conventions but needs significant corrections to be functional.

issues.description: The controller defines a PATCH route at '/' for listing/searching reports, but PATCH is typically used for partial updates, not for list/search operations. The spec likely expects a GET or POST for search with body. This is a spec mismatch in HTTP method.
issues.suggestion: Change @TypedRoute.Patch() to @TypedRoute.Post() for search-with-body, or restructure to use GET with query parameters if the spec supports it.

issues.description: The entire controller file implements project membership CRUD operations, but the provided Prisma schema (Insights namespace) contains no model for project memberships. The schema includes activity logs, reports, snapshots, weekly summaries, budget alerts, and filter tables, but no `hrm_time_tracking_project_memberships` model. Therefore, all endpoints in this controller are phantom endpoints with no basis in the provided ground truth specification.
issues.suggestion: Remove this controller file entirely or verify that the correct Prisma schema (from a different namespace like Projects) was intended for this implementation.

issues.description: The controller implements CRUD for report task filters, referencing model `hrm_time_tracking_report_task_filters`. While this model exists in the provided Prisma schema, the schema is truncated and does not show the complete model definition (the schema ends mid-sentence). However, the controller's operations (POST, PATCH, GET, PUT, DELETE) are plausible for a filter table. Since the schema is incomplete, we cannot verify field-level correctness, but the endpoint type itself is not phantom.
issues.suggestion: No action needed for endpoint existence, but field-level validation requires the complete Prisma schema.

issues.description: The controller method `updateTaskFilters` uses HTTP PATCH at the base path `/hrmTimeTracking/reports/:reportId/taskFilters` with a body of type `IUpdateRequest` and returns a `ICollection`. This appears to be a bulk replacement operation (replace all task filters for a report). The spec comment describes it as 'Update the task filter selection set', which is plausible but the HTTP method PATCH for a bulk replace is unconventional; PUT is more typical for replacement. However, this is a minor spec mismatch, not a hallucination.
issues.suggestion: Consider using PUT for bulk replacement if the OpenAPI spec defines it as such, but this is an implementation detail.

issues.description: Controller uses PATCH method for listing snapshots (index operation) at route '/hrmTimeTracking/reports/:reportId/snapshots'. This is a spec mismatch - listing operations should typically use GET method, not PATCH. The PATCH method is semantically incorrect for retrieving a filtered list.
issues.suggestion: Change @TypedRoute.Patch() to @TypedRoute.Get() for the index method, or if the spec requires PATCH for list operations, ensure this matches the OpenAPI specification.

issues.description: Controller includes console.log(error) statements in catch blocks. While not a hallucination per se, this is implementation detail that should be handled by a global exception filter rather than in controllers.
issues.suggestion: Remove console.log statements and rely on NestJS exception filters for error handling.

issues.description: Controller defines a PATCH route at '/hrmTimeTracking/reports/:reportId/projectFilters' for listing project filters, but PATCH is not a standard HTTP method for collection retrieval. The spec likely expects GET for listing. This is a spec mismatch - using PATCH for read operations contradicts REST conventions and OpenAPI operation definitions.
issues.suggestion: Change @TypedRoute.Patch() to @TypedRoute.Get() for the index method, or verify the OpenAPI spec defines PATCH for this collection retrieval operation.

issues.description: Controller routes '/hrmTimeTracking/owner/timesheets/:timesheetId/approve' and '/hrmTimeTracking/owner/timesheets/:timesheetId/reject' use POST method. While plausible, the spec may define these as PUT or PATCH operations. Without the OpenAPI spec, this cannot be verified as a hallucination but is a potential mismatch.
issues.suggestion: Verify against OpenAPI spec to confirm correct HTTP methods for approval/rejection endpoints.

issues.description: Controller defines route '/hrmTimeTracking/owner/profile' with PUT method, but there is no corresponding OpenAPI path in the provided spec for owner profile updates. The spec only includes Insights models (activity logs, reports, summaries, etc.), not owner profile endpoints.
issues.suggestion: Remove this controller unless there is a corresponding OpenAPI specification for owner profile management.

issues.description: Controller defines routes '/hrmTimeTracking/owner/password-resets' with POST, PATCH, GET, PUT, and DELETE methods, but there is no corresponding OpenAPI path in the provided spec for owner password resets. lines.text: * Validate that the target organization exists. Query the role table for a record matching both the provided organizationId and roleId. Do not perform a role lookup by roleId alone because the requirements require strict organization isolation for role catalogs and access evaluation. If no matching role exists for that organization, return a not-found error.

* Load any normalized permission assignment records associated with the role so the returned DTO can describe the complete permission set of the organization-specific role. Map built-in and custom role characteristics consistently, noting that built-in roles are permanent organization role types while custom roles may be created, updated, and removed when not assigned.

* Return the detailed role DTO. Do not include cross-organization data. Do not mutate any state, create audit changes, or perform reassignment logic in this endpoint. Ensure errors distinguish among unauthenticated access, unauthorized access, missing organization, and missing role within the specified organization.

lines.text: * Check whether any active organization workforce records are still assigned to the role before deletion. If at least one employee assignment exists, reject the request immediately with a business error explaining that the custom role cannot be removed while employees remain assigned. Do not change the role record or its assignment availability in this case.

* When the role is custom, belongs to the specified organization, and has no remaining employee assignments, remove it from the organization's role catalog within a transaction. If the service uses logical deletion consistent with the schema, set `deleted_at` and persist any audit timestamps needed by the platform; otherwise perform the equivalent removal strategy used by the codebase. Return the deleted role representation to the caller.

* Ensure the deletion affects only the current organization. Do not alter built-in roles, role definitions in other organizations, or employee records except for making the deleted custom role unavailable for future assignment. Handle not-found, forbidden, and conflict-style business validation failures deterministically.

lines.text: * This operation registers a new employee authentication identity for the HRM time tracking platform. The underlying actor table, hrm_time_tracking_employees, is described as the authenticated employee account identity store and the parent record for employee authentication flows. The table confirms that the employee signs in with a unique email address and a hashed password, so this endpoint exists to accept employee registration data and create that root authentication record before the employee begins using organization-scoped features such as timelog entry, timesheet work, assigned project access, and personal dashboard usage.

items.message: Class "HrmtimetrackingAuthManagerController" missing JSDoc

lines.text: * This endpoint registers a new manager authentication account for the HRM time tracking platform. The underlying actor table, hrm_time_tracking_managers, is described as storing the login identity and credential state for manager users, and it intentionally contains only the core authentication and lifecycle fields required for access control. In concrete schema terms, that means the registration flow is centered on the manager account email, which is the unique sign-in identifier, and password_hash, which stores the protected credential after secure hashing. The endpoint exists to establish that manager identity before the user can access organization-scoped supervisory capabilities such as employee management, project management, timesheet approval, and report viewing.
